theory.introduction: Quantum entanglement is a phenomenon where multiple qubits become linked in such a way that their states cannot be described independently. The system must be treated as a whole.
key_points: Entangled qubits form a single combined state
The state of one qubit depends on the other
Measurement of one qubit determines the correlated outcome of the other
Entanglement is stronger than classical correlation
It is used in quantum teleportation, cryptography, and computing
theory.intuition: Imagine two perfectly synchronized coins that always match when observed, even though neither had a fixed outcome in advance.

sections.title: What Makes Entanglement Special
content: In classical systems, objects can be correlated but still described separately
In quantum systems, entangled qubits cannot be separated into individual states
The full system must be described together
This is called a non-separable state

sections.title: Mathematical Structure
content: A general 2-qubit state is |ψ⟩ = α|00⟩ + β|01⟩ + γ|10⟩ + δ|11⟩
If it cannot be written as a product of two single-qubit states, it is entangled

sections.title: Bell States
content: Bell states are the simplest examples of maximally entangled states
They represent the strongest possible quantum correlations between two qubits

sections.title: How Entanglement Is Created
content: Start with two qubits in |00⟩
Apply H to the first qubit to create superposition
Apply CNOT to link both qubits
The result is an entangled Bell state

sections.title: Measurement Behavior
content: Measuring one qubit instantly fixes the correlated outcome of the other
If the first qubit is 0, the second is also 0 in |Φ+⟩
If the first qubit is 1, the second is also 1 in |Φ+⟩
Before measurement, neither qubit has a definite classical value

sections.title: Not Faster-Than-Light Communication
content: Entanglement does not let us send information instantly
Measurement outcomes are still random
The correlation appears only after comparing results
This preserves causality

sections.title: Bloch Sphere Limitation
content: Single qubits can be visualized on the Bloch sphere
Entangled states cannot be represented by separate individual Bloch spheres
They live in a higher-dimensional joint state space

examples.title: Create Bell State (Φ+)
examples.description: Apply H and CNOT to create entanglement.

examples.title: Correlation Check
examples.description: Verify that both qubits always match.
examples.note: Even though each outcome is random, the pair is perfectly correlated.

quiz.question: Which gates create entanglement in this lesson?
What results can you get from a Bell state measurement?
